coredata.dc:title: Realization of one-dimensional heat transfer for the study of hygrothermal behavior of insulating material samples

Sidi-Boulenouar, R.
Coussot, P.
coredata.dc:description: Natural materials used for clothes or insulation in construction, like flax, hemp, cotton, wood fibers, etc, are eco-friendly insulators capable of regulating humidity while maintaining high thermal insulation properties. The coupled heat-mass (water) problem remains partially uncontrolled and challenging to simulate. Model experiments must be developed to explore the physics of the process and to validate simulation approaches. In order to solve this problem, we here study the conditions needed for setting up a 1D experiment, i.e., in which the mass and heat transfer occur essentially along one given direction, thus facilitating modelling and comparison with experimental data, while approaching real conditions in walls. This is particularly challenging with such materials exhibiting a low thermal conductivity and being anisotropic, which increases the impact of heat losses along the edge. Moreover, the need for imposing homogeneous boundary conditions along the top surface and for the insertion of thermal sensors prevents using samples of large aspect ratio. In this context, the validity of the 1D heat flow assumption needs to be fully checked for a given geometry. Here, we start by studying in details (experimentally and numerically) the temperature distribution over time during the cooling of cellulose samples of different sizes. The analysis leads to the development of an original approach for quantifying how far we are from 1D conditions along the central axis which consists in determining the virtual thermal conductivity of the material allowing the 1D analytical solution to be in agreement with data. This factor also corresponds to the factor by which the dynamics of cooling are accelerated due to edge effects. The applicability of the method is tested with various other types of insulation materials and under other boundary conditions. Finally, further systematic numerical simulations then provide an appreciation, based on this method, of the distance from 1D heat transport conditions through a simple wall, under various geometrical and boundary conditions. We thus identify general experimental conditions that can be used for the study of 1D heat and mass transfers in biobased construction materials.

head.ce:title: Realization of one-dimensional heat transfer for the study of hygrothermal behavior of insulating material samples

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Laboratoire Navier (Ecole Nationale des ponts et chaussées, Univ. Gustave Eiffel, CNRS), France

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Laboratoire Navier (Ecole Nationale des ponts et chaussées, Univ. Gustave Eiffel, CNRS)

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Laboratoire Navier (Ecole Nationale des ponts et chaussees, Univ. Gustave Eiffel, CNRS), France

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Natural materials used for clothes or insulation in construction, like flax, hemp, cotton, wood fibers, etc, are eco-friendly insulators capable of regulating humidity while maintaining high thermal insulation properties. The coupled heat-mass (water) problem remains partially uncontrolled and challenging to simulate. Model experiments must be developed to explore the physics of the process and to validate simulation approaches. In order to solve this problem, we here study the conditions needed for setting up a 1D experiment, i.e., in which the mass and heat transfer occur essentially along one given direction, thus facilitating modelling and comparison with experimental data, while approaching real conditions in walls. This is particularly challenging with such materials exhibiting a low thermal conductivity and being anisotropic, which increases the impact of heat losses along the edge. Moreover, the need for imposing homogeneous boundary conditions along the top surface and for the insertion of thermal sensors prevents using samples of large aspect ratio. In this context, the validity of the 1D heat flow assumption needs to be fully checked for a given geometry. Here, we start by studying in details (experimentally and numerically) the temperature distribution over time during the cooling of cellulose samples of different sizes. The analysis leads to the development of an original approach for quantifying how far we are from 1D conditions along the central axis which consists in determining the virtual thermal conductivity of the material allowing the 1D analytical solution to be in agreement with data. This factor also corresponds to the factor by which the dynamics of cooling are accelerated due to edge effects. The applicability of the method is tested with various other types of insulation materials and under other boundary conditions. Finally, further systematic numerical simulations then provide an appreciation, based on this method, of the distance from 1D heat transport conditions through a simple wall, under various geometrical and boundary conditions. We thus identify general experimental conditions that can be used for the study of 1D heat and mass transfers in biobased construction materials.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Biobased materials, including traditional wood, paper, and textile materials, as well as novel insulating materials made of wood, flax, hemp, etc [1], have a significant appeal in the construction industry because of their sustainability. They indeed in general allow for a significant reduction of the CO2 emissions associated with the production stage, in particular compared to cementitious materials [2]. The hygroscopic properties of these materials, i.e., their ability to absorb a significant amount of moisture inside the solid phase from vapor in ambient air, a reversible process, also play an important role in various applications as they affect the mechanical properties, durability, and indoor environmental quality. Natural textiles and biobased construction materials are thus considered to improve the comfort of the inhabitants by regulating the ambient humidity [3]. In addition, these materials have good thermal insulation characteristics [4].

Hygrothermal transfers in biobased construction materials and natural textiles are thus of great interest, but they are not yet fully understood. The complexity of their behavior in varying environmental conditions makes it challenging to predict thermal and moisture dynamics accurately. Moreover, the coupling between these two processes makes the physical understanding even more difficult. Sophisticated modelling approaches for hygroscopic building materials [4–17] have also been developed, essentially relying on two governing (diffusion-based) partial differential equations for the moisture content and temperature fields. The predictions of the suggested models are generally compared with macroscopic data obtained under specific conditions. Typically, some ambient RH and/or temperature variations are imposed, and the mass (through weighing) and the temperature and/or the humidity along the external surface of the wall are recorded over time [5,7,10,18–20]. Such data constitute global markers for the moisture and temperature evolutions in the material, but they are not sufficient to ascertain the validity of models that describe in detail the time evolution of vapor, bound water, and temperature distributions inside the material. As a corollary, these data cannot be used to check the validity of the assumptions made concerning the transfer coefficients or to adapt these models.

In order to more completely test the validity of these models and possibly improve them, it is useful to establish simple conditions for heat and moisture fluxes and then track the detailed evolution of the material. Fundamentally, the simplest conditions for that aim are 1D transfers, in which all the averaged transport processes occur mainly along one single direction. Under such conditions, it becomes easier to follow the distributions of temperature and moisture content inside the sample over time and thus validate a model. Moreover, such conditions are close to real conditions for which a wall, of large aspect ratio, is subjected to approximately homogeneous boundary conditions: in that case, transversal transfers are negligible and the transfer process is 1D along the direction perpendicular to the wall.

The modelling approaches of the hygrothermal behavior of building materials are generally done under the assumption of 1D transfers through the walls [21–25]. The most complete works compared the theoretical predictions with experimental data by relying on measurements of temperature and humidity at a few points along a line perpendicular [24–27] or somewhat inclined [22] through the wall, generally in some central location. In this context, 2D transfers and, in particular, the potential impact of heat losses through the edges are not considered, although different geometries have been used, associated with different ratios of the wall thickness to its extent, which could influence the results. In some cases, it has even been considered that a similar 1D flow could be obtained within an almost cubic sample extracted from the wall [25]. Within the frame of this present work, we intend to bring some clarification about the conditions under which a 1D heat and mass transfer flow can be obtained when some piece of material is subjected to some given boundary conditions along one of its surfaces. Our study will thus provide original information about the conditions under which a 1D transfer can effectively be obtained for hygrothermal transfers in a wall, and for studying such transfers at a smaller scale.

In the aim of obtaining a 1D heat transfer process, the most natural approach obviously consists of using a sample of thickness much smaller than its size in the cross-section and imposing uniform boundary conditions along the top and bottom surfaces of the sample. Under such conditions, one naturally reduces the impact of heat losses along the radial and bottom surfaces of the sample, which can be particularly important for insulating materials exhibiting low thermal conductivity (see Ref. [28]). More precisely, heat losses can easily be significant compared to the (relatively low) heat transfers in the main direction. In addition, the anisotropy of these materials, resulting from the compression used for their preparation, may induce a significant anisotropy of the thermal conductivity, the fibers tending to be aligned along the radial direction, which further increases the importance of heat losses along the edges [28]. Also, imposing uniform boundary conditions along large surfaces is challenging. At last, a minimal thickness is required to be able to insert sensors to follow the temperature distribution inside the material. Consequently, it is useful to test relatively thick cylindrical samples of limited cross-sectional areas subjected to controlled, approximately uniform air fluxes along their surfaces. In this context, considering the complexity of obtaining a 1D heat flow (see above: heat losses, anisotropy, large sensor concentration, homogeneous boundary condition), it is crucial to check the validity of this assumption through internal measurements and numerical simulations.

Actually, 1D moisture transfers can more easily be obtained as soon as impermeable covers are applied to all sample surfaces except the top one (see Refs. [29,30]), through which the exchanges with the ambient air can effectively occur, since in that case, no radial transfers at all can occur. It remains that, as for thermal transfers, homogeneous boundary conditions can hardly be imposed along the top surface. However, it seems that, at least for sufficiently slow processes, which in fact correspond to the typical practical situations of hygroscopic transfers resulting from a gradient of humidity, the moisture distribution remains approximately homogeneous in each cross-section, i.e., the transfer is 1D. This could, for example, be observed during the drying of composite systems (a layer of kaolin paste or cellulose paste covering a bead packing [31]) or simple cellulose pastes [32]. To illustrate the point, we show here a series of images of the water distribution during the drying of a cellulose paste in a cylindrical container (see Fig. 1 ), with a similar vertical dry air flux above the sample. Each image provides a view, possibly affected by the local relaxation time, of the total signal along a direction crossing the sample perpendicularly to its main axis, which explains the slight decrease of the signal intensity radially towards the exterior, visible even in the initial (homogeneously) saturated state (see Fig. 1), since due to the cylindrical shape of the sample the total moisture content decreases with the distance from the center. In the next stages, during drying (associated with shrinkage during some period), the water distribution remains approximately homogeneous, even in the final stages, i.e., beyond 230 h, when there is only bound water. Thus, essentially 1D mass transfers are obtained in this kind of geometry, which here corresponds to a maximum thickness of 4 cm for a diameter of 9 cm.

In this context, our plan is to determine the conditions under which heat transfers, when non-coupled with mass transfers, can also be 1D. For such conditions, 1D mass transfers (at constant ambient temperature, i.e., without heat transfers) would be obtained, since we have seen above that 1D transfers are obtained with low aspect ratios. Then, since such conditions allow for getting separately 1D mass transfers and heat transfers, they should also allow to get 1D coupled heat and mass transfers. The conditions for obtaining 1D heat transport are thus sufficient to get 1D hygrothermal transfers as soon as the edges are impermeable.

Thus, here, we intend to clarify the experimental conditions allowing for obtaining a 1D heat transfer. More precisely, we discuss the most appropriate sample geometry and insulation system around the sample in order to get a temperature distribution along the central axis close to the theoretical prediction for a simple 1D heat transfer. In that aim, we start by reviewing the theoretical characteristics of a 1D heat flow under given boundary conditions. Then we compare this analytical solution with experimental data and the predictions of numerical simulations, for two different sample geometries subjected to a given air flux along their surface. This comparison leads us to develop an approach for quantifying the “distance” from the 1D solution, through the factor by which the thermal conductivity has to be multiplied to fit the observed dynamics of the transport. Using this approach, we finally study by numerical simulations the conditions allowing for a 1D heat flow in the case of a wall, through the variations of the correction factor as a function of the aspect ratio of the wall geometry and the relative boundary conditions along the edges and the main surface of the wall. This work thus provides key information concerning the impact of the sample geometry and the boundary conditions on the characteristics of the temperature profile along the central axis of the sample, and on the most appropriate conditions to get a 1D flow in the central region of the wall.

Our model material is made of pure (99.5 %) cellulose fibers (Arbocel BC200, supplied by Kremer Pigmente). The strip-like fibers have an average thickness and width of around 1 μm and 20 μm, and an average length of 300 μm. The fiber density (
ρ s
) is about
1500
kg
m
− 3
, while its heat capacity is
C
p , s
= 1250
J
kg
− 1
K
− 1
[33,34]. For our experiments, we prepare a sample block by compressing a given amount of fibers with a press. The cellulose powder is poured into a plastic mold, then a hydraulic press squeezes the two ends of the specimen tightly. The final product is a cylindrical sample of a specified height and diameter. Note that the fiber preparation and compression were carried out under ambient conditions, which implies that the cellulose initially contains some moisture in the form of bound water absorbed in the amorphous regions between microfibrils (see Ref. [35]). Taking into account the corresponding fraction of bound water and the density of the different phases, the porosity (void to total volume ratio, noted
ε
) was adjusted to 0.5 by controlling the sample volume during compression. van der Waals interactions lead to some bonding between the cellulose fibers, which helps to fix the sample; in other words, the specimen is finally a solid material of density
ρ w
= 750
kg
m
− 3
. From magnetic resonance imaging measurements of the bound water distribution in similar materials, it could be observed that they can at first sight be considered as homogeneous [29]. After compression, the sample is placed in an oven (at 60 °C) to dry completely. Since in the next steps of the experiment, the surfaces of the sample open to air will be subjected to dry air flux, this ensures that it will remain dry and that there will be no influence of humidity on our measurements, a critical point with hygroscopic materials like cellulose.

To determine the thermal conductivity of the cellulose specimen, we employed a heat flow meter (HFM-25) from Thermtest, a device specifically designed for testing small samples of insulation and building materials. During the experiment, the two plates of the instrument establish a controlled temperature gradient across the sample, enabling us to calculate the thermal conductivity by measuring the heat flow through the material. To determine the thermal conductivity of our material in both axial and radial directions, we conducted measurements using specifically prepared samples. For the axial direction, where the heat flux is parallel to the direction of compression, cylindrical samples with a diameter of 7 cm and a thickness of 1 cm were used. These dimensions were chosen to minimize edge effects while ensuring consistent and reliable measurements. To measure the radial thermal conductivity, where the heat flux is perpendicular to the direction of compression, the preparation process involved reorienting the sample. The cellulose material was compressed and then reconfigured (rotated) to produce a square sample with dimensions of 7.5 × 7.5 cm and a thickness of 1 cm. In this case, the direction of compression was deliberately aligned perpendicular to the heat flux to capture the anisotropic behavior of the material. For both orientations, the thermal conductivity was calculated using Fourier's law, based on the heat flux values directly measured by the Heat Flow Meter (HFM) device. Note that for this apparatus, a preliminary calibration stage with standard materials of well-known conductivity led us to correct our conductivity data by dividing by a factor of 1.15 applied to any measurement, whatever the experimental conditions, which likely finds its origin in imprecise control of the exact temperature imposed at the plate/sample interface. Under these conditions, we obtained the axial conductivity (
k z
) a value of
∼ 0.075
W
m
− 1
K
− 1
and for the radial conductivity (
k r
) a value of
∼ 0.16
W
m
− 1
K
− 1
, this difference reflecting the influence on heat transfer of material structure and fiber alignment along some specific axis resulting from the sample preparation by compression. These values are consistent with the typical values obtained for cellulose or nanocellulose fiber stacks [36–38] or dry biobased materials used for insulation of buildings and made by compression of natural (cellulosic) plant fibers [39,40].

Two geometries of sample and setup are used (see below), and the sample top surface is subjected to a vertical stream of dry air (see Figs. 2 and 3
). The interest of a cylindrical geometry is that all edges are thus at equal distance from the central axis, which facilitates the analysis of the validity of the 1D assumption. However, note that, as will be seen later, our conclusions about the aspect ratio of the sample beyond which we can get a 1D heat transfer along the central axis may be extrapolated to a sample of square cross-section. The velocity of this airflow (at room temperature, i.e., 20 °C–24 °C) can be adjusted through the air supply system. The pipe from which the air arrived has a diameter of 4 mm, and the air outlet is placed at a distance of 2.5 cm from the surface of the sample. This system induces an approximately homogeneous radial flow of air along the sample surface. Except when mentioned, the velocity of the air flux was 20 L/min. To reduce the heat losses through the sample sides and bottom, the rest of the sample is surrounded (see Figs. 2 and 3) by Polyurethane (PU) foam material with a lower thermal conductivity than cellulose (
0.025
W
m
− 1
K
− 1
). We have a priori good contact between our samples and insulation material, but even if there remains some thin air layer, we cannot expect significant air flux in such enclosed and dead-end regions, so that the heat transfers will still essentially rely on conduction through a material (air) of conductivity almost equal to the conductivity of the insulating material. Thus, this layer will contribute to insulation in the same way as the insulating material.

Given these hygroscopic systems, we verified that, under the conditions of these experiments, heat transfer dominated the internal evolution within the sample, while mass transfer remained negligible. Initially, the cellulose samples were dried, and the air flux used was cleaned and dried with desiccant air dryers. Finally, by monitoring the system's mass throughout the experiment, we confirmed that the sample's mass variation remained below 1 %, indicating negligible vapor absorption during the test.

Temperature variations within the sample were monitored in real-time using Type T thermocouples, composed of copper and constantan (a copper–nickel alloy). These sensors were embedded at precisely controlled depths by drilling into the sample at predefined locations. This setup enabled us to capture the temporal evolution of temperature along the sample thickness (z-axis), which we refer to as a one-dimensional (1D) temperature distribution. Thermocouples store data every second through a computer-connected device called a data logger. Note that to ensure consistency and reliability of the results, each experiment was performed twice under identical conditions. The maximum local differences were smaller than 8 %.

In the first configuration (Geometry I, see Fig. 2), we used a cylindrical specimen with a diameter of 5.0 cm and a height of 5.2 cm. Thermocouples were positioned along the central axis at 1.0 cm intervals (see Fig. 5), enabling straightforward monitoring of the temperature profile through the sample thickness. In the second configuration (Geometry II, see Fig. 3), a wider specimen (7.0 cm diameter, 2.0 cm height) was surrounded by a thicker polyurethane insulation layer. Due to the reduced height and increased fragility of the sample, thermocouple placement required careful optimization: sensors needed to be spaced far enough apart to minimize mutual thermal interference and avoid structural damage, yet close enough to the central axis to ensure representative measurements. Under these constraints, one thermocouple was placed at the free surface and another at the bottom. The remaining sensors were positioned at successive heights every 0.25 cm above the bottom (with the first at 0.5 cm), offset radially by 0.2 cm from the center and arranged in a staggered pattern with 60° angular separation (see Fig. 4
).

The experiment starts by placing the entire system (sample + insulation foam + thermocouples) into the oven till it reaches the initial temperature
T 0
. Thus, when starting the experiment, the whole setup (including the cellulose specimen with the foam insulation layer) will be considered as one block cooled to a room temperature of about 24 °C. Note that the temperature was still controlled inside the oven by keeping the connection with the thermocouples. Then the entire system is removed and immediately placed in the room and subjected to the air flux at ambient temperature (see above). The temperature given by the different thermocouples is then recorded while the sample is cooling. From such a set of data, i.e., the temperature over time at each position in the sample (see Fig. 5a), it is possible to deduce the distribution of temperatures along the thickness over time (every second) by representing, for each time, the temperature at the different positions in the sample (see Fig. 5b).

From such data, we can also estimate the total heat loss from the sample at time t, i.e.,
E t
. Indeed, the current energy stored in the form of heat in the sample writes
Q
( t )
= ρ

is the sample volume. The amount of heat that has left the sample at the time
t
is then determined as the difference between the initial energy of the system and the energy remaining in the sample at that time:
E t
= Q
( 0 )
− Q
( t )
.

To quantify the reliability of our experimental measurements and derived quantities, we applied the method of propagation of error as described by Ref. [41]. This approach accounts for both systematic and random errors and enables estimation of uncertainty in derived parameters based on the uncertainties of the measured inputs. For measured quantities, uncertainties arise from the measurement method and are determined by the equipment used (see Table 1 ). For the derived quantities, this value cannot be directly taken from the measurement; it has to take into account the error from the equipment and operating procedure. Uncertainties in derived quantities are based on the Moffat method:

is a characteristic length of the sample surface, typically the surface extent in the direction
r
. The factor
h =
k a
∗ f
(
Re , Pr
)
/
L c
being an unknown parameter, is named the convection heat transfer coefficient. This factor
h
obviously depends on the heat diffusion coefficient into the air and on the exact characteristics of the airflow, considering the specific boundary conditions (geometry of the setup). It basically depends on the (external) fluid flow properties along the surface, but also depends on the material properties through the roughness of its interface with air, which affects the flow characteristics at a short distance from the surface. However, this dependence does not seem to have been studied in depth in the literature. Also note that the different parameters involved in the Prandtl and Reynolds numbers negligibly vary in the range of variation of the temperature during our tests. Consequently, as long as we maintain the same air flow, the boundary condition for heat transfer may be expressed with the expression (4) with a constant value for
h
, and the resulting heat flux along the boundary thus varies only if
T s
varies. We have a similar situation for the external surfaces of the insulating material, now with a boundary condition being imposed by the ambient air convection in the room, which we can consider as constant on average. Note that the values for
h
are a priori unknown, but we will show later that it is possible to estimate them from some of our experiments with Geometry II (see below and Appendix 1). The obtained values were used for these simulations.

Actually, the calculation leading to the formal equation (4), i.e., the expression of the boundary condition as a function of dimensionless numbers and temperature gradient between the surface and the incoming flux, is obtained through a rigorous fluid mechanics approach and from an integral over the solid surface, but under the assumption of a unique surface temperature value all along this surface. However, due to the advection-convection process in the air along the sample surface and its coupling with the thermal transfers in the material, we can anticipate that the surface temperature is not perfectly uniform along this surface. In order to check that point, we measured the surface temperature at different radial distances. The data show that the temperature decreases by a few degrees from the central point on the surface to the periphery. It is nevertheless difficult to take into account this effect in the theory. Instead, since we observe that
T s
−
T ∞
varies by about 20 % around its average value, the coefficient
h
deduced experimentally from the use of the expression (4) with the surface temperature at the central point, which is the smallest one, can be considered as an average coefficient overestimating the average value of
h
along the surface by about 20 %. Considering this limited difference and the fact that other 2D effects inside the sample (radial heat transport) might partly compensate the impact of this effect, we neglect this aspect here, but we note that it should be considered carefully for experiments with large walls subjected to air fluxes reaching them on a specific point.

For the simulations, the governing equations are discretized spatially using finite element method with a Galerkin procedure, while temporally employing the implicit backward Euler method. The mesh resolution was selected to ensure a balance between computational efficiency and accurate field representation. The time step is adaptive according to the convergence rate, ensuring stability and accuracy in capturing the dynamics of heat transfer. The temperature variation over time at different locations of the specimens was obtained after solving the model. Simultaneously, the heat transfer across the various surfaces of the cellulose sample is calculated, with further details to be elaborated upon later.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Theory for 1D heat transfer in the sample
ce:section.ce:para:
In this study, we focus on a specific case where heat transfer within the sample can be reasonably approximated as a one-dimensional (1D) process. This assumption is justified by the sample geometry, wherein the cross-sectional area is sufficiently large relative to its thickness, effectively minimizing lateral heat losses. Under these conditions, edge effects are considered negligible, and thermal transport is assumed to occur predominantly along the z-axis. In this scenario, the heat equation (2) becomes:
(5)

Let us now analyze these results and, in particular, appreciate how they are influenced by the values of the different parameters. In equations (6) and (8), only two factors directly affect the heat transfer characteristics: the thermal diffusivity of the material
α s
and the parameter L. The dimensionless parameter
L
corresponds to the ratio of some characteristic heat transfer (say, e.g.,
h
T a
, in which
T a
is some arbitrary temperature) along the interface as a result of the external air flux, to a characteristic heat transfer (say, e.g.,
k
T a
/ l
) due to thermal conduction in the sample. Finally, the main characteristics of the evolution of the temperature distribution over time, i.e., the successive shapes of the temperature profiles, directly depend on the value of
L
, while the dynamics, i.e., the times at which the different distribution shapes are obtained, solely depend on
α s
. Indeed, for sufficiently small
L
, the heat diffusion is fast compared to the heat extraction due to air flux, so that the temperature has time to equilibrate through the sample (see Appendix 2, Figure A1a) and the temperature, while decreasing, remains homogeneous in the sample (the variations as a function of
z
in (6) are negligible). In contrast, for large
L
, the temperature does not have time to equilibrate, and we have a process governed by the internal diffusion of heat (the diffusivity controls the heating rate). This leads to curved temperature profiles over time (see Appendix 2, Figure A1b,c,d). In that case, for a sufficiently large value of
L
, the boundary conditions no longer affect the heat conduction inwards. To sum up, the evolution of the shape of the temperature distribution over time solely depends on the value of
L
. However, the dynamics, i.e., the times at which the different profiles of temperature are obtained, depend on the value of
α s
(see expression (6)). Thus, the temperature distribution over time is a straightforward marker of the heat transfer process in the material. We deduce a possible approach to analyze the data: we can fit the value of
L
onto the profile shape evolution, and then compare the dynamics, i.e., the different times at which these profiles are obtained, with the theoretical predictions. To complete the information, we can also follow
E t
/
E ∞
for several values of
L
(see Appendix 2, Figure A2), which illustrates how the dynamics are governed.

We first look at data obtained with geometry (I) (see Fig. 2). The temperature distribution over time obtained from our measurements is represented in Fig. 6 . As expected, the temperature decreases over time until it reaches the ambient one, but this process occurs by propagation from the top surface: the temperature profiles are curved at the approach of the free surface (see Fig. 6) and the ambient temperature is finally reached later at larger depth. This confirms that the air flux induces a significant heat flux through the top layer, which progressively cools the sample in depth. Note that beyond 1080s the bottom layer seems to cool faster than the upper layer, an effect which may result from an insufficient insulation of the sample bottom. We will discuss this point further below.

Let us now compare these data with the predictions of the analytical solution in the pure 1D case. We choose the most appropriate value for L fitting the evolution of the shape of the temperature distribution according to equation (6), i.e.,
L = 22
(see Fig. 6). The agreement found in that case concerning the successive temperature profiles proves that the model is valid in terms of its predictions of the qualitative characteristics of the process. As a next step, we can fit the value of
α
through the dynamics of the system, by determining the most appropriate value for representing the data for the total energy (computed from the integral of the temperature distribution, see Section Experimental procedure) with the help of equation (8). Here, this leads to
α = 3.2 ×
10
− 7
m 2
s
− 1
. The ability to predict the dynamics of the process with these two parameter values (
L , α
) further appears in the agreement of the data for the temperature distribution over time with the theoretical prediction (see Fig. 6).

Upon examining the expression for the thermal diffusivity
α
as a function of material properties, we find that achieving a value of
3.2 ×
10
− 7
m 2
s
− 1
would require a thermal conductivity significantly higher than the independently measured value for the cellulose sample in the vertical direction (see Table 2), approximately 4 times greater. This substantial discrepancy between the experimental observations and the analytical solution suggests that the effective thermal behavior observed in the experiment cannot be explained solely by the intrinsic material properties. Instead, it indicates that additional heat losses, likely due to edge effects, played a significant role. Furthermore, the analytical model, using the measured vertical thermal conductivity, predicts a total cooling time exceeding 15 h, whereas the actual experiment concluded in just 3.9 h. This results in a time prediction error of approximately 3.85, which closely matches the factor by which the thermal conductivity would need to be increased to reconcile the analytical model with the observed dynamics.

We now evaluate the ability of three-dimensional numerical simulations to replicate the experimental observations. As detailed in the Methods section, these simulations account for full 3D heat transfer and incorporate the actual thermal conductivity values of the materials. The results demonstrate excellent agreement with the experimental temperature profiles along the central axis (see Fig. 7 ). Both the shape of the temperature distributions and their temporal evolution are accurately captured, confirming that the simulation reliably reproduces the dynamics of heat propagation observed in the experiments.

In light of the observed agreement between experimental data and simulation results, it is instructive to examine the full 3D temperature field predicted by the numerical model. As illustrated in Fig. 8 , the temperature decreases in a spatially homogeneous manner, forming an approximately spherical cooling region around the sample. This spatial pattern confirms that the heat transfer process is inherently three-dimensional rather than strictly one-dimensional. Consequently, temperature gradients emerge in multiple directions, giving rise to significant heat fluxes not only along the vertical axis but also laterally and radially. These findings indicate the presence of non-negligible heat losses along the sample edges, thereby challenging the validity of the 1D assumption employed in the simplified analytical model.

It is instructive to look in more detail at these heat losses that can be deduced from the simulations. In that aim, we integrate the heat flux through a surface (i.e.,
q = − k
(
∇ T
)
s
) over the top surface, the bottom surface, and the lateral surface of the sample, to get the total amount of heat lost per unit time. We can see (see Fig. 9 ) that during the first stage, the rate of heat loss along the sample top is much larger than along the other surfaces. This is due to the fact that the strongest contrast of temperature along the sample surface is found along the top surface, due to the air flux. In the next stage, say, after about 1000 s, the heat loss along the different surfaces has become of the same order, and they will go on evolving in the same way. This confirms that a large fraction of heat is lost through the sample edges, complicating the study of unidirectional heat transfer. Therefore, in order to get a pure 1D flow, modifying the sample geometry is essential.

Let us now consider the results in terms of temperature distribution over time obtained under similar air flux conditions but now with Geometry II (see Fig. 10 ). The evolution of this distribution is qualitatively similar to that observed with Geometry I. Now, we can well represent the shape evolution of the temperature distribution with the 1D model by taking
L ≈ 28
(see Fig. 10), It is then possible to correctly predict the dynamics with
α = 9.12 ×
10
− 8
m 2
s
− 1
. In that case, the thermal conductivity would be
0.085
W
m
− 1
K
− 1
, i.e., only equal to the independently measured conductivity multiplied by a factor of 1.14. This correction factor is consistent with the time discrepancy between the analytical prediction and the experimental duration. The model, using vertical thermal conductivity, predicts a cooling time of 2.2 h, while the experiment completes in 1.92 h, yielding a ratio of approximately 1.15, which closely matches the correction factor.

Actually, here, the numerical simulations, which take into account the edge effects, are not in perfect agreement with the data but are in close agreement with the 1D model predictions without any factor of correction of the conductivity (see Fig. 11 a). This confirms that in this geometry, there is a negligible impact of heat loss along the edges on the evolution of the temperature along the central axis. This is confirmed by the aspect of the 3D temperature distribution over time (see Fig. 11b). Indeed, throughout the majority of the process duration, the temperature gradients in the radial and angular directions are observed to develop primarily outside the sample volume. As a result, the temperature distribution within each cross-section remains nearly uniform, which is characteristic of a one-dimensional heat transfer regime. Furthermore, as illustrated in Fig. 11b, the heat loss through the top surface consistently dominates over lateral and bottom losses, reinforcing the assumption that vertical heat transfer is the primary mechanism governing the thermal evolution of the sample.

Although numerical simulations suggested a strong agreement between the one-dimensional theoretical predictions and experimental data, the observed discrepancies point to additional heat loss mechanisms not previously considered. A probable source of heat losses is the thermocouples themselves, which are made of copper, a material with high thermal conductivity. These sensors may act as localized heat sinks, extracting heat from the sample and thereby distorting the temperature field (See below).

A 3D simulation was subsequently conducted to evaluate the effect of the copper thermocouple wires on the heat transfer in the sample. As revealed by previous simulations, heat losses along the sides of the cellulose sample are negligible due to the presence of a thick insulating foam. Therefore, in this simulation, we merely focus on the cellulose sample itself, applying a zero-heat flux on the sides. This simplification maintains the accuracy of the simulation but considerably reduces the computational time. The same governing model, simulation method, and similar boundary conditions on the top open surface are used in this 3D simulation. However, the various components (cellulose and copper wires) were assigned their respective material property parameters. Since the same cellulose is used in the experiments, the material properties of cellulose, including density, thermal conductivity, and specific heat capacity, remain consistent with previous simulations. The copper thermocouple wire is modeled as a plain cylinder with a diameter of 0.45 mm, a thermal conductivity of 400 W m−1 K−1, a density of 8960 kg m−3, and a specific heat capacity of 390 J kg−1 K−1. Additionally, fine meshes were applied at the interface between the cellulose fiber and thermocouple wire to ensure the accuracy of the simulation results.

Under these conditions, the results of this simulation are now in excellent agreement with the experimental data (see Fig. 12 ) by using
α = 8 ×
10
− 8
m 2
s
− 1
(Table 2). This value of alpha is now consistent with the measured conductivity. Thus, unlike the previous analytical solutions and simulation approaches without considering the thermocouple's effect, no additional factors or adjustments are required to get this agreement. This underscores the improved accuracy and reliability of the simulation and confirms that the experiment is affected by the temperature difference due to the thermocouple.

It is also useful to explore how the thermocouples act, and in particular if they induce localized distortion of the temperature field, in particular along the central axis, close to their tips, or a global distortion. In the pictures below (see Fig. 13 ) showing the simulated temperature field in a sample cross-section including two thermocouples (see Fig. 13), one can see how the thermocouples affect the temperature distribution. The temperature is significantly affected by the thermocouples, but this disturbance extends all along their length (see Fig. 13). Such a disturbance explains their global impact on the dynamics (see the above discussion about the comparison with the data and analytical solution). There is, however, apparently no significant distortion of the temperature field along the central axis, close to the thermocouple tips. In order to check that point, we computed the discrepancy between the temperature along the central axis as predicted by the 1D analytical solution (with the correction on the conductivity indicated above) and the value obtained from these simulations. We found that in the different positions along the central axis, this discrepancy is always less than 8 % of the temperature difference between the top and the bottom.

Finally, note that we successfully tested the validity of the simulations and the applicability of the method under different boundary conditions (different air flux intensity) or initial conditions (different initial temperature) (see Appendix 3).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Applicability of the method for other materials
ce:section.ce:para: In this section, we test the applicability of our methodology to materials exhibiting different thermal conductivities, namely a Polyurethane foam (PU foam), which also serves as the insulation layer in this study, and a Polytetrafluoroethylene (PTFE) sample, commonly known as Teflon. These materials were chosen not only for their widespread use in thermal systems but also for their contrasting physical characteristics (see Table 2), which allow for a meaningful comparison.

Experimental measurements were carried out under the conditions of Geometry II, starting from samples at 60 °C, with thermocouples introduced at different positions in the samples, simulations were carried out incorporating the thermal influence of the thermocouples for greater accuracy, and the 1D analytical solution for one-dimensional heat transfer was calculated. The differences in the dynamics and detailed characteristics of the profiles observed for different materials and air fluxes result from the different boundary conditions (see below) and material characteristics, but it is out of the scope of the present study to discuss further these aspects. The main point in the present context is the good agreement between the simulations, the analytical solution, and the experimental data for the PTFE and for the PU foam (see Figs. 14 and 15). This confirms that our analysis and approach developed in this paper is sufficiently robust to be used for this range of materials. A critical point to emphasize here is that for these materials, in contrast with the cellulose, no conductivity correction factor was necessary, i.e., using the real conductivity of the material as determined independently was sufficient to get a good agreement between the different theoretical predictions and the data. Such a result was obtained because, in contrast with the strongly anisotropic cellulosic sample, these materials are essentially isotropic.

ce:section.ce:section-title: How far we are from ideal 1D heat transport conditions in transfer experiments with a wall
ce:section.ce:para: Through the above results, we were able to appreciate the importance of heat losses along the edges of a sample, and more precisely, how they perturb the expected theoretical 1D transport characteristics. These conclusions were obtained under specific but fully controlled conditions, i.e., a sample enclosed in the volume of insulating material with only one of its side exposed to an approximately uniform external air flux. In practice, the experiments aimed at studying the hygrothermal behavior of biobased materials are often carried out with walls exposed to some specific conditions of humidity and temperature on their two main sides, while the conditions along the edges are not controlled. Actually, our detailed study of the two specific cases provided a new method for quantifying the characteristics of the temperature distribution inside the wall, and more precisely, a method to characterize how far we are from a simple 1D flow. Indeed, we showed that the heat losses along the edges do not affect the shape of the temperature distribution inside the sample but rather the dynamics of variation of the internal heat, or equivalently, the timing of variation of the temperature profiles in the material. We also showed that the proper dynamics, observed in the experiments and in the simulations, can also be obtained from the 1D analytical solution by using a virtual thermal conductivity. This process, in fact, allows for artificially enhancing the dynamics in a way that is sufficient to get an excellent agreement between the 1D analytical solution and the data or simulations along the central axis. In this context, the factor by which the conductivity is increased is a quantity characterizing the distance from a 1D heat flow. A factor approaching 1 means that the experimental conditions are close to the ideal ones for obtaining a 1D flow along the central axis.

This approach enables quantification of deviations from one-dimensional heat flow in the central region of a wall exposed to external air fluxes on multiple faces. More precisely, we consider a square or cylindrical solid wall, of length or diameter
D
and of thickness
l
. Thus, the geometry of the wall is characterized by its aspect ratio, i.e.,
l / D
. We then assume that there is no heat flux through one of the main faces of the wall, and that it is subjected to a uniform air flux along its opposite main face. In practice, in particular in a climatic chamber, the validity of this assumption depends on the way the air is mixed inside the chamber. This boundary condition is finally described with the help of the expression (4), with a coefficient
h =
h
s u r f a c e
. Moreover, we assume that the edges of the wall are subjected to a uniform air flux, leading to a similar boundary condition with a coefficient
h
e d g e s
. The ratio
a / b =
h
s u r f a c e
/
h
e d g e s
thus quantifies the relative importance of the air fluxes along the different faces of the wall. A value of
a / b
tending to zero would correspond to a wall whose edges are surrounded by a solid insulating material.

Numerical simulations are conducted to evaluate the temperature distribution within the wall during a typical test, in which the material is initially at 60 °C and subjected to ambient temperature air flow. Note that this simulation uses the same theory of 3D heat transfer (see above). For simplicity, the geometry was primarily modeled as cylindrical. However, to assess the influence of geometry, additional simulations were carried out using a square cross-section (see Fig. 16
), showing that the difference with the cylindrical case is negligible in our range of tests. Lastly, it is important to note that we assume the material to be anisotropic in the same way as our cellulose sample, i.e., with a thermal conductivity of 0.16
W
m
− 1
K
− 1
along the transverse direction and 0.075
W
m
− 1
K
− 1
along the longitudinal direction, perpendicular to the wall surface. This situation is particularly appropriate for describing insulating biobased materials made by compression of fibers or plant pieces, which tend to orient along the direction of the wall plane (perpendicular to the direction of compression).

Looking at the results (see Fig. 16), we can see that the correction conductivity factor increases when the ratio
a / b
tends to 1. This is expected, since a larger ratio means that the air fluxes along the edges are more efficient to extract heat from the sides, i.e., heat losses increase. Also, for a given value of
a / b
, the conductivity correction factor approaches 1 when the aspect ratio
l / D
tends to zero. More specifically, when the ratio
b / a
tends to zero, corresponding to negligible air flux along the lateral edges, a 1D approximation will remain valid for any aspect ratios. Such a result is obviously obtained because both cases (
b / a = 0
or
l / D ≪ 1
) correspond to negligible edge effects. In contrast, for
a / b
in the order of 1, i.e., when the air flux conditions are similar along all faces, a 1D flow can be obtained only for an aspect ratio smaller than 0.1. Thus, the critical aspect ratio for which a 1D flow is obtained, i.e., for which the correction conductivity factor remains below 1.15, increases when
a / b
increases (see Fig. 16). The main analysis focuses on the case with
h
s u r f a c e
= 100
W
m
− 2
K
− 1
.

Additional trends were observed by varying the boundary conditions. For a lower air flux such that
h
s u r f a c e
= 10
W
m
− 2
K
− 1
(while keeping the same value for
a / b
), the correction factor
β
is somewhat lower than for the higher flux (see Fig. 16).

Finally, we also carried out simulations assuming that the wall edges are surrounded by an insulating material layer, of width (around the wall) and thickness both equal to the wall thickness, and along which the temperature is assumed to remain constant and equal to the ambient one. Under these conditions, when the air flux is low
h = 10
W
m
− 2
K
− 1
, the evolution of the correction factor closely resembles that of the case with two air fluxes at
a / b = 10
with
h
s u r f a c e
= 100
W
m
− 2
K
− 1
. Conversely, for high flux
h = 100
W
m
− 2
K
− 1
, the correction factor evolution aligns more closely with the case of two fluxes at
a / b = 75
with
h
s u r f a c e
= 100
W
m
− 2
K
− 1
. Such variations naturally imply variations of the critical aspect ratio below which a 1D flow can be obtained. In our range of tests, this critical ratio ranges from 0.15 to 0.6. This means that a 1D heat flow, as expected from the real material properties, can be obtained along the central axis of the wall for a relatively large aspect ratio, whatever the boundary conditions.

This work contributes to clarifying the conditions under which one can expect a 1D flow in the central region of a bio-based wall. In the first part of the work, through the comparison of experimental data with internal measurements, analytical approach, and numerical simulations, for two different geometries under well controlled conditions, we developed a method to appreciate quantitatively how far we are from 1D flow conditions in the central region of a sample. This original approach is based on the observation that even if the theoretical 1D conditions do not apply due to significant heat losses through the edges, the heat flow characteristics in the central region can still correspond to the theoretical predictions if a larger conductivity of the material is assumed. The conductivity correction factor thus quantifies how far we are from the 1D conditions, and it was shown that it also essentially corresponds to the factor by which the dynamics of cooling is accelerated due to the heat losses along the edges.

Equipped with this method, we could thus show that the conditions to obtain a 1D heat transport in the center of a wall depend on the aspect ratio of this wall but also the boundary conditions along the edges compared to those along the main wall surface. These exact conditions were established in the context of the study of bio-based wall for constructions that exhibit a low and anisotropic thermal conductivity, and subjected to different air fluxes along their faces. In the case of samples enclosed in solid insulating materials except for one surface subjected to an air flux we showed that we can expect a 1D flow for an aspect ratio smaller than 2/7. Note that although we did not study this point systematically, our data for other systems prove that this limit is larger for isotropic materials with different conductivities and usually employed as insulating systems.

Through this analysis of the different configurations and comparison with experimental data, we could prove in this study the validity of the numerical simulations in accurately predicting the characteristics of the temperature evolution in various insulating materials over time, under different boundary conditions. Moreover, we showed that the predictions can be relevantly refined by taking into account the heat transfers through the thermo-sensors.

Through this study of the appropriate conditions for 1D flow, we actually developed several approaches which appear useful in view of future studies of the hygrothermal behavior of bio-based materials. Indeed, we showed the possibility of following the temperature distribution inside the material and how the presence of thermosensors can affect the heat flow characteristics. In this way, we developed a technique for determining the total heat energy stored in the material (by integration of the internal heat along the sample thickness) without the need to measure the heat flux through the free surface of the material. Thus, we avoid the potential local strong perturbation of the boundary condition due to the presence of the sensor for measuring the heat flux. Our detailed comparison of the different approaches for measuring or predicting the temperature distribution also proved the consistency of the approaches as soon as all perturbating effects are taken into account, and boundary conditions are properly quantified, all aspects which appear particularly sensitive in the context of bio-based materials exhibiting low thermal conductivity.

V.T. Nguyen: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Visualization, Methodology, Data curation, Conceptualization. Y. Zou: Visualization, Methodology, Data curation. K. Mourda: Methodology. R. Sidi-Boulenouar: Supervision, Conceptualization. P. Coussot: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix 1
ce:section.ce:para:
Boundary conditions play a crucial role in determining the accuracy of calculations and simulations in this study. In the results section, specifically for model geometry (II), we estimated the value of the parameter
L
by fitting the theoretical 1D analytical solution to the shapes of the experimental temperature distributions at different times (let us recall that only the shape is taken into account at this stage, not the dynamics). For the case of cellulose, the resulting agreement is relative, as it was shown that, to precisely fit the dynamics, it should be finalized by assuming a slightly larger thermal conductivity (by 14 %) than the real one. The numerical simulations relying on this value and considering all the heat losses confirmed the validity of this conclusion. We thus could deduce that the cellulose samples for an air flux rate of 20 L/min, we have
h = 120
W
m
− 2
K
− 1
, for 1 L/min,
h = 40
W
m
− 2
K
− 1
, and for room conditions (with conditioned air)
h = 27
W
m
− 2
K
− 1
. Note that, considering the relatively slow variation of the shapes of the successive temperature profiles for large values of
L
(see above), its value determined by fitting the model to data, can be considered to be found with an uncertainty of about 15 %, and this is the same for the resulting values for
h
. Following the same procedure, slightly different values were found for the PTFE samples, i.e.,
35
W
m
− 2
K
− 1
for 1 L/min to
185
W
m
− 2
K
− 1
for 20 L/min, but significantly different values were found for the PU foam, i.e.,
20
W
m
− 2
K
− 1
for 1 L/min and
30
W
m
− 2
K
− 1
for 20 L/min, which results from the different roughnesses of the materials, but it is out of the scope of this paper to elaborate further on this point.

Note that we simplified the treatment by assuming that the temperature along the external surface of the isolating foam is constant and equal to the room temperature (23 ± 1 °C), which considering the radial thickness of the isolating foam layer, is likely an excellent approximation.

ce:section-title.ce:bold: Temperature distribution and energy evolution for 1D analytical solution

ce:section.ce:para:
We present here the main aspects of the temperature distribution for different values of
L
in the case of the 1D analytical solution (see Figure A1), and the corresponding total energy evolution over time (see Figure A2).
Fig. A1
Analytical solution in the case of a pure 1D heat transfer (see text) for different values of

: (a) 0.1, (b) 3, (c) 10, (d) 50, with
α = 8 ×

.
Fig. A1
Fig. A2

L

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix 3
ce:section.ce:section-title: Further tests under different boundary or initial conditions
ce:section.ce:para:
A similar test was also performed, but with a much lower air flux velocity (1 L/min). The simulation was carried out taking into account the impact of thermocouples while keeping all other parameters identical. The comparison with the simulation results demonstrates a strong agreement (Figure A3). Between 500 s and 2000 s, a minor discrepancy appears in the temperature measurements near the top surface of the specimen, which may be due to the relatively low airflow velocity, potentially allowing the inner layers of the specimen to reach thermal equilibrium. Nonetheless, the simulation maintained a high level of accuracy, as this thermal equilibrium was achieved after a sufficiently long period. Note that the correction factor
β = 1.15
can be used to fit the dynamics of this case.
Fig. A3
Temperature distribution (symbols) at different times during the experiment with geometry (II) with air flux speed of 1 L/min and comparison with the predictions of a simulation (continuous lines) taking into account heat losses due to thermocouples.
Fig. A3

Furthermore, simulations were carried out for another case where the initial temperature of the specimen was set to 40 °C, while the airflow velocity was 20 L/min. The results, shown in Figure A4, demonstrate once again the predictive capability of the simulations. This finding confirms that the model is not only reliable for scenarios with an initial temperature of 60 °C but also adaptable to other temperature conditions, further validating its applicability across a range of experimental setups. In this case,
β = 1.2
can be used to fit the dynamic transfer.
Fig. A4
Temperature distribution (symbols) at different times during experiment with geometry (II) with air flux speed of 20 L/min and initial temperature of 40 °C, and comparison with the predictions a simulation (continuous lines) taking into account heat losses due to thermocouples.
Fig. A4
